topic: Religion & Ethics: Applied Ethics (Euthanasia, Business Ethics, Sexual Ethics)
subject: religious-studies

items.q: Is withdrawing futile treatment the same as euthanasia in UK law?
items.correct: No - withdrawing burdensome or futile treatment in a patient's best interests is lawful medical practice, while deliberately administering a lethal dose remains illegal.
items.misconception: Doctors who withdraw treatment are committing euthanasia under UK law.
items.random_wrong: All forms of euthanasia are legal in the UK.
items.why: Students collapse the act/omission and legal distinctions that the euthanasia debate is actually about.

items.correct: No - the calculation could go either way: relief of suffering counts in favour, but effects on families, trust in doctors and slippery-slope risks count against; the theory fixes a method, not a verdict.
items.misconception: A utilitarian must always approve a request for euthanasia.
items.why: Mapping theories to fixed answers loses the application marks; the same theory can generate opposed conclusions from different facts.

items.q: Is whistle-blowing always an act of disloyalty?
items.correct: Not necessarily - Kantian honesty and stakeholder arguments hold that preventing serious harm can override loyalty, and exposing wrongdoing may serve the firm's genuine long-term interests.
items.misconception: Employees are morally bound to stay silent about wrongdoing out of loyalty.
items.random_wrong: Whistle-blowing refers to refereeing company sports matches.
items.why: Framing loyalty as absolute hides the conflict of duties that makes whistle-blowing a rich applied-ethics case.

items.q: What is the difference between active and passive euthanasia?
items.correct: Active involves a deliberate act to end life, such as a lethal dose; passive involves withdrawing or withholding treatment
items.misconception: Active is legal in the UK while passive is not
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Active involves a deliberate act to end life, such as a lethal dose; passive involves withdrawing or withholding treatment. “Active is legal in the UK while passive is not” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What does the sanctity of life principle claim?
items.correct: Life is intrinsically valuable and God-given, so it should not be deliberately ended
items.misconception: Life's value depends entirely on its usefulness
items.why: Correct answer: Life is intrinsically valuable and God-given, so it should not be deliberately ended. “Life's value depends entirely on its usefulness” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What did Milton Friedman argue about business?
items.correct: A business's social responsibility is to increase its profits within the rules of the game
items.misconception: Businesses must prioritise charity over profit
items.why: It's A business's social responsibility is to increase its profits within the rules of the game. “Businesses must prioritise charity over profit” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What does the doctrine of double effect permit?
items.correct: An action with a foreseen bad side effect, if the intended effect is good and proportionate - such as pain relief that may shorten life
items.random_wrong: Deliberate killing if the motive is kind
items.why: It's An action with a foreseen bad side effect, if the intended effect is good and proportionate - such as pain relief that may shorten life. “Any action with two effects” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
